content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Every application stores boolean values.
CAN_EDIT = true
IS_MODIFIED = false
IS_EDITED = true
USE_AFTER = falseAt first glance, this seems perfectly fine. But what happens when you need to store dozens of such flags for billions of records?
Instead of storing each flag individually, you can pack all of them into a single integer using bit flags.
This technique has been used for decades in operating systems, databases, game engines, network protocols, and high-performance applications because it’s incredibly memory-efficient and fast.
Let’s see how it works.

What are Bit Flags?
A bit flag uses each individual bit inside an integer to represent one boolean value.
A binary number:
00000000contains 8 bits.
Each bit can be either:

• 0 → false

• 1 → true
Instead of storing eight separate booleans, we can store all eight inside a single byte.
For example:
Bit Position7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0
0 0 1 0 1 1 0 1Suppose these represent:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

If the value is
00101101then:

• Can Read ✅

• Can Delete ✅

• Is Admin ✅

• Premium User ✅
Everything else is false.

Why Use Bit Flags?
Imagine storing permissions.
Without bit flags:
{
"canRead": true,
"canWrite": false,
"canDelete": true,
"isAdmin": true,
"premium": false,
"verified": true
}That’s six separate boolean values.
With bit flags:
flags = 45where
45 = 00101101One integer replaces multiple booleans.

Assigning Bit Values
Each flag gets a unique power of two.
Read = 1 (00000001)
Write = 2 (00000010)
Delete = 4 (00000100)
Admin = 8 (00001000)
Premium = 16 (00010000)
Verified = 32 (00100000)Notice only one bit is set in each value.

Combining Flags
Use the OR operator (|).
Java:
int permissions = READ | DELETE | VERIFIED;Binary:
00000001
00000100
00100000
---------
00100101Result:
37Now 37 stores three permissions.

Checking a Flag
Use AND (&).
if ((permissions & DELETE) != 0) {
System.out.println("Delete allowed");
}Why?
00100101
00000100
---------
00000100Non-zero means the bit exists.

Adding a Flag
Suppose you want to add Admin permission.
permissions |= ADMIN;Before:
00100101After:
00101101Only that bit changes.

Removing a Flag
Use:
permissions &= ~ADMIN;Example:
Before
00101101Admin mask
00001000Invert
11110111AND
00100101Admin is removed.

Toggling a Flag
Use XOR (^).
permissions ^= PREMIUM;If Premium was OFF → it becomes ON.
If Premium was ON → it becomes OFF.

Java Example
public class PermissionDemo { static final int READ = 1;
static final int WRITE = 2;
static final int DELETE = 4;
static final int ADMIN = 8; public static void main(String[] args) { int permissions = READ | DELETE; permissions |= ADMIN; if ((permissions & DELETE) != 0) {
System.out.println("Delete allowed");
} permissions &= ~READ; System.out.println(permissions);
}
}
Memory Savings
Suppose you have:

• 32 boolean flags

• 1 billion records
Without bit flags:
32 × 1,000,000,000 = 32,000,000,000 bytes ≈ 29.8 GiB ≈ 32 GBUsing one integer:
4 bytes/record
4 × 1,000,000,000 = 4,000,000,000 bytes ≈ 3.73 GiB ≈ 4 GBThat’s an 87.5% reduction in storage for this simplified example. Actual savings depend on the language, object layout, and serialization format.

Real-World Uses
Bit flags are everywhere.
Operating Systems
File permissions
Read
Write
ExecuteLinux uses bit masks extensively.

Game Development
Character abilities
Invisible
Flying
Poisoned
ShieldedOne integer tracks them all.

Networking
TCP packets use bits for flags like:

• SYN

• ACK

• FIN

• RST
A few bits describe the packet state.

Databases
Instead of storing:
isDeleted
isVerified
isPremium
isAdminStore:
status = 41Decode whenever needed.
Conclusion
Bit flags are one of the simplest yet most effective optimization techniques available.
They let you:

• Store dozens of boolean values in a single integer

• Reduce memory usage

• Improve cache efficiency

• Perform fast permission and state checks using bitwise operations
Whether you’re building a database schema, a game engine, an operating system, or a high-performance backend service, bit flags are a valuable tool to keep in your programming toolbox.
